perturbation.original_text: The obligation under this Agreement shall continue for an indefinite period from the date of disclosure of Information. In the event of dispute as to the date of actual disclosure the Information, this shall be deemed to have been disclosed on the date this agreement is signed.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligation under this Agreement shall continue for a period of 2 years from the date of disclosure of Information. In the event of dispute as to the date of actual disclosure the Information, this shall be deemed to have been disclosed on the date this agreement is signed.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text limits the confidentiality period to 2 years. Some trade secrets can retain value for longer periods and might be considered misappropriated if disclosed even after this contractual period. The change creates a conflict because the contract's limited protection period could be insufficient to protect trade secrets under UK law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 2 defines trade secrets and provides legal avenues for their protection. By limiting the confidentiality period to 2 years, the modified contract may not adequately protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under UK law, as the economic value of such trade secrets may extend beyond this period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides an indefinite period for confidentiality, whereas the changed contract limits this to 2 years. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this limited period may not adequately protect trade secrets under UK law, particularly if their economic value extends beyond 2 years. The scraped law snippet is about access requests to FederalRegister.gov, which does not directly contradict the text. However, the Law Explanation specifies that the modified contract may not adequately protect information that qualifies as a trade secret, which is inconsistent with the protections afforded to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement expresses the entire Agreement and understanding between the parties and supersedes any previous understandings, commitments or agreements, oral or written, pertaining to the subject matter of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement expresses the entire Agreement and understanding between the parties, but it does not supersede any previous oral agreements, pertaining to the subject matter of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a conflict with the principle of 'entire agreement clauses' under English law. By stating that the agreement does not supersede prior oral agreements, it creates uncertainty and potential enforceability issues, contradicting the purpose of such clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 3 concerns implied terms, the presence of which this clause attempts to mitigate. By contradicting the purpose of an entire agreement clause and leaving the door open to prior agreements, the modified clause potentially undermines the certainty and enforceability intended by English contract law.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 3 / CHAPTER 1 / § 3 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 3 USC 3 : Number of electors Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 3-THE PRESIDENT CHAPTER 1-PRESIDENTIAL ELECTIONS AND VACANCIES Jump To: Source Credit §3. Number of electors The number of electors shall be equal to the number of Senators and Representatives to which the several States are by law entitled at the time when the President and Vice President to be chosen come into office; except, that where no apportionment of Representatives has been made after any enumeration, at the time of choosing electors, the number of electors shall be according to the then existing apportionment of Senators and Representatives. ( June 25, 1948, ch. 644, 62 Stat. 672 .)
perturbation.justification: The original text includes an entire agreement clause, which is standard in contracts. The changed text explicitly states that the agreement does not supersede prior oral agreements. This undermines the entire agreement clause, creating a direct contradiction by stating that prior oral agreements are still valid, which goes against the original clause's intent to supersede all prior agreements. Although the provided snippets do not directly relate, the Perturbation Explanation states that this alteration "creates uncertainty and potential enforceability issues, contradicting the purpose of such clauses," and the Law Explanation notes that the change "potentially undermines the certainty and enforceability intended by English contract law."

perturbation.original_text: ONS makes no warranty regarding the accuracy or completeness of the Information provided hereunder, an d accepts no responsibility for any expenses, losses or action incurred or undertaken by the RECIPIENT as a result of the receipt or use of such Information.
perturbation.changed_text: ONS makes no warranty regarding the accuracy or completeness of the Information provided hereunder, and accepts no responsibility for any expenses, losses or action incurred or undertaken by the RECIPIENT as a result of the receipt or use of such Information, even in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct by ONS.
perturbation.explanation: This attempts to exclude liability even for gross negligence or willful misconduct. English law generally doesn't allow excluding liability for these types of actions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 states that a person cannot exclude or restrict his liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. Although the modified clause relates to information accuracy rather than personal injury, attempting to exclude liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct is likely to be deemed unreasonable and therefore unenforceable under the Act.
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to exclude liability even for gross negligence or willful misconduct, which the perturbation explanation states is generally not allowed under English law. While the provided law snippet is missing, the perturbation explanation and the LLM law explanation both indicate that attempting to exclude liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct is problematic, creating a legal contradiction because it undermines potential legal accountability for those actions.

perturbation.original_text: The RECIPIENT agrees to comply fully with all relevant export laws of the United Kingdom to assure that neither the Information nor any direct product thereof are (i) exported, directly or indirectly, in violation of Export Laws; or (ii) are intended to be used for any purposes prohibited by the Export Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: The RECIPIENT agrees to comply with all relevant export laws of the United Kingdom to assure that neither the Information nor any direct product thereof are (i) exported, directly or indirectly, in violation of Export Laws; or (ii) are intended to be used for nuclear proliferation purposes only.
perturbation.explanation: This modified text limits the prohibited uses to nuclear proliferation, essentially allowing the information to be used for other purposes prohibited by export laws. This clearly contradicts the intention of export control regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the Export Control Order 2008 prohibits the export of controlled goods without a license. By limiting the prohibited uses of the information, the modified contract allows for potentially illegal exports that would otherwise be restricted under this Order, directly contradicting its purpose.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: UK strategic export controls - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Guidance UK strategic export controls The UK's regulatory framework for strategic export controls and the circumstances where you might need an export licence. From: Export Control Joint Unit , Department for International Trade and Department for Business and Trade Published 19 December 2022 Last updated 21 November 2023 — See all updates Get emails about this page Print this page This guidance is for those who export or transfer in goods, software or technology (including data, information and technical assistance) which might be subject to strategic export controls. It explains what control lists are, as well as who they apply to and when, so that exporters can make sure they comply with the law. It is a criminal offence to export controlled items without the correct licence. Control lists The UK maintains a list of all items that are subject to strategic export controls. This is known as the consolidated list of strategic military and dual-use items that require export authorisation . The list includes items that are controlled because of international agreements or arrangements as well as further items relating to specific defence or security concerns of the UK . The two main categories of goods are: military goods , software and technology that are specially designed or modified for military use dual-use items which are goods, software and technology that can be used for both civilian and military applications Searching the consolidated control list It is an exporter’s responsibility to check whether items require an export licence. If so, you need to apply well in advance of shipment to avoid any potential issues (such as customs problems at a port). To find out if an item is listed you can: check the consolidated list of strategic military and dual use items use the OGEL and goods checker tools use the ‘control list classification service’ or the ‘end-user advice service’ in SPIRE Who export controls apply to Strategic export controls apply to anyone exporting or transferring goods, software or technology, or providing brokering services. Read about export controls applying to academic researchers for guidance on exemptions. When export controls apply You must apply for a licence from the Export Control Joint Unit ( ECJU ) if any of the following apply: your items are on the consolidated control list you have concerns, or you have been informed of concerns about the intended end-use or the end-user your items are covered by trade sanctions End-use controls The government has powers to require an export licence for items even if they are not on the consolidated control list. These are generally referred to as ‘catch-all’ or ‘end-use’ controls. Military end-use controls Military end-use controls may apply if there is a risk that the item is intended for military use in a destination that is subject to an arms embargo. Go to guidance on end-use controls applying to military related items WMD and WMD technical assistance end-use controls WMD end-use controls may apply if there is a risk that an item may be intended or diverted for purposes connected with WMD or their means of delivery. The controls relate to the provision of technical assistance. Technical assistance includes instructions, skills, training, working knowledge, consulting services and the transfer of technical data. It covers the supply, delivery, manufacture, maint
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by limiting the prohibited uses of the information to 'nuclear proliferation purposes only.' This directly conflicts with the broader scope of export control laws, which aim to prevent the export of controlled items without a license for various reasons, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The original text ensures compliance with 'all relevant export laws,' while the changed text narrows this scope, potentially allowing exports for other prohibited purposes. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that 'It is a criminal offence to export controlled items without the correct licence' and refers to a 'consolidated list of strategic military and dual-use items that require export authorisation'. By restricting the prohibited uses, the modified contract allows actions that the export control laws seek to prevent, thereby establishing a contradiction. The perturbation explanation accurately identifies that it contradicts the intention of export control regulations.
